# US Army TC 25-20, A Leader's Guide to After-Action Reviews (deep reference)

**Source:** Headquarters, Department of the Army (1993). *A Leader's Guide to After-Action Reviews*. Training Circular 25-20. Washington, DC, 30 September 1993. Distribution Restriction: "Approved for public release; distribution is unlimited." Proponent: U.S. Army Combined Arms Command, Deputy Commanding General for Training (ATTN ATZL-CTT, Fort Leavenworth, KS 66027-7000). Authentication: GORDON R. SULLIVAN, General, United States Army, Chief of Staff; MILTON H. HAMILTON, Administrative Assistant to the Secretary of the Army. Licence: **Public domain (17 USC §105 — US Government work product).**
**Scope:** open
**Structure:** Five chapters plus appendix and glossary. Preface; Ch 1 *The After-Action Review* (definition, types, planning/execution sequence); Ch 2 *Planning the After-Action Review* (AAR plan, selecting OCs, reviewing T&E plan, scheduling stopping points, attendance, sites, training aids); Ch 3 *Preparing for the After-Action Review* (review doctrine, identify key events, observe and take notes, collect observations, organise observations, select/reconnoitre site, prepare site, rehearse); Ch 4 *Conducting the After-Action Review* (introduction and rules, review of objectives and intent, summary of recent events, discussion of key issues, optional issues, force protection, closing comments); Ch 5 *Following Up* (benefits, immediate retraining, revised SOPs, AAR in combat); Appendix A *After-Action Review Techniques* (site selection, site improvement, training aids, organisation of the AAR site); Glossary; References (FM 25-100 *Training the Force*, November 1988; FM 25-101 *Battle Focused Training*, September 1990).
**Citation style:** `(Ch N, "Section name")`. The PDF converted cleanly via `pymupdf4llm 0.2.9` in 3.2 s with page anchors preserved, but section names are used in citations because they are the more durable anchor across re-conversions.
**Coverage:** full source read end-to-end (35 PDF pages / 52 KB of markdown). No partial-coverage caveat.

## Author's thesis (paraphrased from source)

The Training Circular's organising claim is that AARs are the keystone of the Army's training-evaluation process. The Preface defines the function: "After-action reviews (AARs) help provide soldiers and units feedback on mission and task performances in training and in combat. After-action reviews identify how to correct deficiencies, sustain strengths, and focus on performance of specific mission essential tasks list (METL) training objectives" [V] (Preface). Chapter 1's load-bearing definition specifies the AAR's distinctive feature: it is "a professional discussion of an event, focused on performance standards, that enables soldiers to discover for themselves what happened, why it happened, and how to sustain strengths and improve on weaknesses" [V] (Ch 1, "Definition and Purpose"). The discovery framing is the crux — soldiers *discover for themselves* rather than being told.

A second load-bearing claim is that the AAR is not a critique. Chapter 4 states the constraint directly: "An AAR is not a critique. No one, regardless of rank, position, or strength of personality, has all of the information or answers" [V] (Ch 4, "Introduction and Rules"). The TC contrasts AARs with critiques on epistemic grounds: "Because soldiers and leaders participating in an AAR actively discover what happened and why, they learn and remember more than they would from a critique alone. A critique only gives one viewpoint and frequently provides little opportunity for discussion of events by participants. Soldier observations and comments may not be encouraged. The climate of the critique, focusing only on what is wrong, prevents candid discussion of training events and stifles learning and team building" [V] (Ch 1, "Definition and Purpose"). The TC also states that the AAR "does not grade success or failure" because "[t]here are always weaknesses to improve and strengths to sustain" [V] (Ch 4, "Introduction and Rules").

A third load-bearing claim is that the *spirit* and *climate* of the AAR matter as much as its procedure. The Preface frames this explicitly: "Key is the spirit in which AARs are given. The environment and climate surrounding an AAR must be one in which the soldiers and leaders openly and honestly discuss what actually transpired in sufficient detail and clarity that not only will everyone understand what did and did not occur and why, but most importantly will have a strong desire to seek the opportunity to practice the task again" [V] (Preface). Chapter 4 carries this through to the leader's behaviour during the AAR: "Soldier participation is directly related to the atmosphere created during the introduction" [V] (Ch 4, "Introduction and Rules"), with explicit techniques (enter the discussion only when necessary; reinforce that disagreement is permissible; use open-ended and leading questions; focus on learning) [AP] (Ch 4, "Introduction and Rules").

A fourth load-bearing claim is that AARs are a four-step planning-to-following-up process — not an event but a cycle. Chapter 1 states: "The AAR process has four steps: Step 1. Planning; Step 2. Preparing; Step 3. Conducting; Step 4. Following up (using AAR results)" [V] (Ch 1, "After-Action Review Planning and Execution Sequence"). Chapters 2 through 5 are organised one-per-step, and Chapter 5 is explicit that "[t]he real benefits of AARs come from taking the results and applying them to future training" [V] (Ch 5, "Benefits"). The cycle closes only when retraining occurs.

## Part I: Definition and types of AAR (Ch 1)

### The AAR as professional discussion focused on standards

The TC's working definition is: "An after-action review (AAR) is a professional discussion of an event, focused on performance standards, that enables soldiers to discover for themselves what happened, why it happened, and how to sustain strengths and improve on weaknesses" [V] (Ch 1, "Definition and Purpose"). The AAR provides three things: "Candid insights into specific soldier, leader, and unit strengths and weaknesses from various perspectives; Feedback and insight critical to battle-focused training; Details often lacking in evaluation reports alone" [V] (Ch 1, "Definition and Purpose").

The AAR is positioned as the *keystone* of evaluation: "Evaluation is the basis for the commander's unit-training assessment. No commander, no matter how skilled, will see as much as the individual soldiers and leaders who actually conduct the training. Leaders can better correct deficiencies and sustain strengths by carefully evaluating and comparing soldier, leader, and unit performance against the standard. The AAR is the keystone of the evaluation process" [V] (Ch 1, "Definition and Purpose"). Feedback is defined operationally: "Feedback compares the actual output of a process with the intended outcome. By focusing on the task's standards and by describing specific observations, leaders and soldiers identify strengths and weaknesses and together decide how to improve their performances. This shared learning improves task proficiency and promotes unit bonding and esprit" [V] (Ch 1, "Definition and Purpose").

The TC is explicit that AARs are not panaceas: "Of course, AARs are not cure-alls for unit-training problems. Leaders must still make on-the-spot corrections and take responsibility for training their soldiers and units. However, AARs are a key part of the training process. The goal is to improve soldier, leader, and unit performance. The result is a more cohesive and proficient fighting force" [V] (Ch 1, "Definition and Purpose").

### AAR versus critique

The contrast with critiques is structural, not stylistic. The TC argues that critiques produce inferior learning because they collapse perspectives:

> "Because soldiers and leaders participating in an AAR actively discover what happened and why, they learn and remember more than they would from a critique alone. A critique only gives one viewpoint and frequently provides little opportunity for discussion of events by participants. Soldier observations and comments may not be encouraged. The climate of the critique, focusing only on what is wrong, prevents candid discussion of training events and stifles learning and team building."
> (Ch 1, "Definition and Purpose")

This positions the AAR as epistemically distinct: a critique transmits a single observer's view; the AAR aggregates and tests views across all participants.

### Formal versus informal AARs

All AARs follow the same general format and focus on improving training proficiency. The distinction is in resource intensity and preparation: "How leaders conduct a particular AAR determines whether it is formal or informal. A formal AAR is resource-intensive and involves the planning, coordination, and preparation of supporting training aids, the AAR site, and support personnel. Informal AARs (usually for soldier, crew, squad, and platoon training) require less preparation and planning" [V] (Ch 1, "Types of After-Action Reviews"). Formal AARs are planned at the same time as the near-term training plan (six to eight weeks before execution) and may require site reconnaissance, training-aid coordination, and observer/controller selection and training (Ch 1, "Formal").

Formal AARs are usually held at company level and above, with squad and platoon AARs held *before* higher-echelon formal AARs so that lower-level observations can be brought up: "This allows all levels of the unit to benefit from an AAR experience. It also provides OCs and leaders with observations and trends to address during the formal AAR" [V] (Ch 1, "Formal").

Informal AARs are conducted for soldier and small-unit training at platoon level and below, and can also be used at company and battalion level when resources for a formal AAR (including time) are unavailable. They use the standard AAR format. The TC's worked example shows informality literally: "after destroying an enemy observation post (OP) during a movement to contact, a squad leader could conduct an informal AAR to make corrections and reinforce strengths. Using nothing more than pinecones to represent squad members, he and his soldiers could discuss the contact from start to finish" [V] (Ch 1, "Informal"). Informal AARs also feed upward: "during lower echelon informal AARs, leaders often collect teaching points and trends they can use as discussion points during higher echelon formal AARs" [V] (Ch 1, "Informal").

The most significant difference: "informal AARs require fewer training resources and few, if any, training aids. Although informal AARs may be part of the unit evaluation plan, they are more commonly conducted when the leader or OC feels the unit would benefit. Providing immediate feedback while the training is still fresh in soldiers' minds is a significant strength of informal AARs" [V] (Ch 1, "Informal").

### Four-step AAR process

The TC frames the AAR as a process, not an event: "The AAR process has four steps: Step 1. Planning; Step 2. Preparing; Step 3. Conducting; Step 4. Following up (using AAR results)" [V] (Ch 1, "After-Action Review Planning and Execution Sequence"). After-action review planning is "a routine part of unit near-term planning (six to eight weeks out)" during which leaders "assign OC responsibilities and identify tentative times and locations for AARs. This ensures the allocation of time and resources to conduct AARs and reinforces the important role AARs play in realizing the full benefit of training" [V] (Ch 1, "After-Action Review Planning and Execution Sequence").

## Part II: Planning the AAR (Ch 2)

### The AAR plan as foundation

"A key element in an evaluation plan is the AAR plan. The AAR plan provides the foundation for successful AARs. Leaders develop an AAR plan for each training event" [V] (Ch 2, "The After-Action Review Plan"). The AAR plan contains five elements: "Who will observe the training and who will conduct the AAR; What trainers should evaluate (training and evaluation outlines (TEOs)); Who is to attend; When and where the AAR will occur; What training aids trainers will use" [V] (Ch 2, "The After-Action Review Plan").

Figure 2-1 in the TC shows a worked exercise AAR plan with seven fields: Observer (1Lt Jones), Element (1st plt), Priority tasks (Occupy, prepare, and defend a battle position), Who attends (All), When held (1 hour after contact broken), Location (Behind 2d squad GH44319218), Special requirements (LTC Smith will provide closing comments) [V] (Ch 2, Figure 2-1).

The TC distinguishes informal and formal *evaluations* alongside informal and formal *AARs*. "Informal evaluations are most commonly used at battalion level and below. They are Conducted by all leaders in the chain of command; Continuous; Used to provide immediate feedback on training proficiency" [V] (Ch 2, "The After-Action Review Plan"). "Formal evaluations are usually scheduled on the long-range and short-range calendars. These include ARTEP evaluations, expert infantry badge (EIB), expert field medic badge (EFMB), and technical validation inspections (TVIs). They are Sometimes unannounced, such as an emergency deployment readiness exercise (EDRE); Normally highlighted during quarterly training briefs (QTBs) and yearly training briefs (YTBs); Resourced with dedicated evaluators or OCs" [V] (Ch 2, "The After-Action Review Plan"). External evaluations are conducted by the headquarters two levels above the unit being evaluated: "division evaluates battalion; brigade evaluates companies; battalion evaluates platoons; and company evaluates sections, squads, teams, or crews" [V] (Ch 2, "The After-Action Review Plan").

### Selecting and training observers and controllers

Observers and controllers (OCs) are the human infrastructure of the AAR. The selection criteria: OCs should be people who "Can perform the tasks to be trained to Army standards; Are knowledgeable on the duties they are to observe; Are knowledgeable on current TTPs" [V] (Ch 2, "Selecting and Training Observers and Controllers"). When using external OCs, "trainers must ensure that OCs are at least equal in rank to the leader of the unit they will evaluate. If trainers must choose between experience and understanding of current TTPs or rank, they should go with experience. A staff sergeant with experience as a tank platoon sergeant can observe the platoon better than can a sergeant first class who has no platoon sergeant experience" [V] (Ch 2, "Selecting and Training Observers and Controllers").

OCs should not have other duties that distract from observation. When that is impossible, "leaders in the chain of command should evaluate subordinate units and conduct the AARs" — squad leaders evaluate soldiers in their squads, platoon leaders or sergeants evaluate squads, company commanders or first sergeants evaluate platoons, and so on, with the constraint that "[i]f possible, they should avoid evaluating their own duties and tasks. (It is hard to be objective about your own performance and to determine how it will affect your unit.)" [V] (Ch 2, "Selecting and Training Observers and Controllers").

OC training is itself a load-bearing concern: "Trainers must train their small-unit leaders and OCs. Each OC leads AARs for the element he observes and provides input to the AAR leader for the next higher echelon. Leaders and OCs must be trained in the use of the methods, techniques, and procedures in this training circular" [V] (Ch 2, "Selecting and Training Observers and Controllers"). The TC recommends apprenticeship: "If possible, trainers should assign someone with AAR experience to accompany and assist an inexperienced AAR leader until he is proficient" [V] (Ch 2, "Selecting and Training Observers and Controllers"), and adds that "[t]he trainer must *conduct AARs to help AAR leaders improve their performances*" [V] (Ch 2, "Selecting and Training Observers and Controllers"). Inexperienced AAR leaders should observe properly conducted AARs before attempting to lead one. The trainer must include classes on small-group discussion techniques in OC instruction.

### Stopping points, attendance, sites, and training aids

Stopping points are scheduled: "Leaders must schedule time to conduct AARs as an integrated part of overall training. When possible, they should plan for an AAR at the end of each critical phase or major training event" [V] (Ch 2, "Scheduling Stopping Points"). For planning, "leaders should allow approximately 30-45 minutes for platoon-level AARs, 1 hour for company-level AARs, and about 2 hours for battalion-level and above. Soldiers will receive better feedback on their performance and remember the lessons longer if the AAR is not rushed" [V] (Ch 2, "Scheduling Stopping Points"). The TC adds a flow caveat: "Reviewers must fully address all key learning points. They must not waste time on dead-end issues" [V] (Ch 2, "Scheduling Stopping Points").

Attendance is governed by the AAR plan: "Normally, only key players attend. At times, however, the more participants present, the better the feedback" [V] (Ch 2, "Determining Attendance"). At squad and platoon levels, everyone should attend and participate. At company or higher, it may not be practical to have everyone attend because of continuing operations or training; in that case, "friendly and OPFOR commanders, unit leaders, and other key players (fire support team (FIST) chief, radio telephone operator (RTO), and so on) may be the only participants" [V] (Ch 2, "Determining Attendance").

Site selection: the AAR "will usually occur at or near the training exercise site" [V] (Ch 2, "Selecting Potential AAR Sites"). Pre-positioned training aids and rapid assembly of key personnel minimise wasted time. "Ideally, the AAR site should allow soldiers to see the terrain where the exercise took place. If this is not possible, the trainer should find a location which allows them to see the terrain where the most critical or significant actions occurred. If needed, the trainer should have a terrain model or enlarged map or sketch and a copy of the unit's graphics so everyone can relate key events to the actual terrain" [V] (Ch 2, "Selecting Potential AAR Sites"). Soldier comfort is operational, not optional: "by removing helmets and so on, providing shelter from the elements (sun, cold, rain, snow), having refreshments (coffee, water), and creating an environment where participants can focus on the AAR without distractions. Participants should not face into the sun, and key leaders should have seats up front. Vehicle parking and equipment security areas should be far enough away from the AAR site to prevent distractions" [V] (Ch 2, "Selecting Potential AAR Sites").

Training aids: "Training aids should directly support discussion of the training and promote learning" [V] (Ch 2, "Choosing Training Aids"). Figure 2-2 separates aids appropriate for formal AARs (terrain model, enlarged map, models, dry-erase marker board, photographs, video camera and monitor) from aids appropriate for informal AARs (unit markers, pointer, unit maneuver graphics, communications recordings, rocks and twigs, colored chalk) [V] (Ch 2, Figure 2-2). The bottom line: "use a training aid if it makes the AAR better" [AP] (Ch 2, "Choosing Training Aids"). The TC provides a selection checklist: "What points will I need to make during the AAR, and what training aids will help me make these points? Will the aid illustrate one or more of the main points? Can I use the actual terrain or equipment? Does the aid have any restrictions or special requirements, such as additional generators? Will participants be able to see and hear it? Is it really necessary for the discussion or just nice to have?" [V] (Ch 2, "Choosing Training Aids").

The TC closes the planning chapter with a caution about changes to the AAR plan:

> "Remember that every change takes preparation and planning time away from subordinate OCs or leaders. This may impact the quality of feedback. The purpose of the AAR plan is to allow OCs and AAR leaders as much time as possible to prepare for the AAR. Frequent or unnecessary changes prevent that."
> (Ch 2, "Reviewing the AAR Plan")

## Part III: Preparing for the AAR (Ch 3)

### Reviewing doctrine, training objectives, orders, and METL

"Preparation is the key to the effective execution of any plan. Preparing for an AAR begins before the training and continues until the actual event" [V] (Ch 3, "Review Doctrine, Training Objectives, Orders, and METL"). OCs review current doctrine, technical information, and applicable unit SOPs in advance to be "tactically and technically proficient" [V] (Ch 3, "Review Doctrine, Training Objectives, Orders, and METL"). They also review the unit's training objectives, orders (OPORDs and FRAGOs), and METL: "The unit's training objectives focus on the specific actions and events which OCs must observe to provide valid observations and to effectively lead the unit in its discussion during the AAR. … The METL contains the complete task, conditions, and standards for each task" [V] (Ch 3, "Review Doctrine, Training Objectives, Orders, and METL").

### Identifying key events

OCs must focus their observations on actions required to perform tasks to standard and accomplish training objectives. The TC lists six examples of critical events: "Issuance of OPORDs and FRAGOs; Troop-leading procedures (TLPs); Contact with opposing forces; Resupply and reconstitution operations; Intelligence preparation of the battlefield (IPB); Passage of lines" [V] (Ch 3, "Identify Key Events Observers and Controllers Are to Observe").

### Observing the training and taking notes

"All unit activities have three phases: planning, preparation, and execution. These phases can help the OC structure his observation plan and notetaking. He should keep an accurate written record of what he sees and hears and record events, actions, and observations by time sequence to prevent loss of valuable information and feedback" [V] (Ch 3, "Observe the Training and Take Notes"). The OC can use any recording system "(notebook, prepared forms, 3-by-5 cards) that fits his needs as long as it is reliable, sufficiently detailed (identifying times, places, and names), and consistent. The OC could also use a small portable tape recorder, but he should not get carried away with gadgets when a pencil and paper would do" [V] (Ch 3, "Observe the Training and Take Notes"). Each observation should carry a date-time group (DTG) so observations can be cross-integrated across OCs to give "a comprehensive and detailed overview of what happened" [V] (Ch 3, "Observe the Training and Take Notes").

Figure 3-1 specifies a sample observation worksheet with the fields: Training/exercise title; Event; Date/time; Location of observation; Observation (player/trainer action); Discussion (tied to task and standard if possible); Conclusions; Recommendations (indicate how the unit could have executed the task(s) better or describe training the unit will need to improve future performances) [V] (Ch 3, Figure 3-1).

The OC's positioning is itself a skill: "One of the most difficult OC tasks is to determine when and where to position himself to observe training. The OC does not always need to stay close to the unit leader. Sometimes he can see more from locations where he can observe the performance of critical tasks or the overall flow of unit actions. However, he should not position himself where he would be a training distracter. He must look and act as a member of the unit (using individual and vehicle camouflage, movement techniques, cover and concealment, and so on). He must not compromise the unit's location or intent by being obvious. At all times, he should be professional, courteous, and low-key" [V] (Ch 3, "Observe the Training and Take Notes"). Communications nets can be monitored as an additional observation channel: "Modern technology can quickly record radio transmissions using voice-activated tape recorders or video cameras. By listening to radio traffic, OCs can trace the dissemination of orders and messages as well as monitor information flow from subordinate units. When appropriate, OCs can monitor computer traffic on the Maneuver Control System (MCS) to determine unit actions or status and to identify the impact of inaccurate information on unit operations" [V] (Ch 3, "Observe the Training and Take Notes").

### Collecting observations from other OCs and the OPFOR

The AAR leader needs a complete picture of the training: "Therefore, each OC must give him input. This input may come from subordinate units, combat support (CS) and combat service support (CSS) units, or adjacent units" [V] (Ch 3, "Collect Observations from Other Observers and Controllers"). The OPFOR perspective is explicitly included: "The leader may also receive input from OPFOR leaders, players, and OCs. The enemy's perspective is often useful in identifying why a unit was or was not successful. During formal AARs, the OPFOR leader briefs his plan and intent to set the stage for a discussion of what happened and why" [V] (Ch 3, "Collect Observations from Other Observers and Controllers").

### Organising observations, site selection, site preparation, and rehearsal

The leader puts notes in chronological sequence "so he can understand the flow of events. Next, he selects and sequences key events in terms of their relevance to training objectives, identifying key discussion and/or teaching points" [V] (Ch 3, "Organize Observations"). The leader selects sites near where the training occurred and reconnoitres alternate sites. The AAR site is set up so participants can see the actual terrain or training aids: "Horseshoe arrangements encourage discussion and allow everyone to see" [V] (Ch 3, "Prepare the AAR Site"). Pre-positioning of training aids and equipment is recommended where possible.

After thorough preparation, "the leader reviews the AAR format (Figure 1-2), rehearses at the AAR site, and gets ready to conduct the AAR. He should then announce to unit leaders the AAR start time and location. He must allow enough time for OCs to prepare and rehearse while unit leaders account for personnel and equipment, perform actions which their unit SOP requires, and move to the AAR site" [V] (Ch 3, "Rehearse").

## Part IV: Conducting the AAR (Ch 4)

### Introduction and rules

The AAR begins with an "attention getter" — "a joke, an appropriate anecdote, or a historical example that relates to the training, exercise, event, or conduct of the AAR" [V] (Ch 4, "Introduction and Rules"). The leader then reviews purpose and sequence to ensure everyone understands what an AAR is and how it works. The TC specifies three things the introduction should establish:

> "An AAR is a dynamic, candid, professional discussion of training which focuses on unit performance against the Army standard for the tasks being trained. Everyone can, and should, participate if they have an insight, observation, or question which will help the unit identify and correct deficiencies or maintain strengths. An AAR is not a critique. No one, regardless of rank, position, or strength of personality, has all of the information or answers. After-action reviews maximize training benefits by allowing soldiers, regardless of rank, to learn from each other. An AAR does not grade success or failure. There are always weaknesses to improve and strengths to sustain."
> (Ch 4, "Introduction and Rules")

Figure 4-1 specifies the recommended sequence: Introduction and rules → Review of objectives and intent (training objectives; Commander's mission/intent; OPFOR commander's mission/intent; relevant doctrine, tactics, techniques, and procedures) → Summary of recent events (what happened) → Discussion of key issues (chronological order of events; battlefield operating system (BOS); key events/themes/issues) → Discussion of optional issues (soldier/leader skills; tasks to sustain/improve; statistics; others) → Discussion of force protection (safety) → Closing comments (summary) [V] (Ch 4, Figure 4-1).

"Soldier participation is directly related to the atmosphere created during the introduction. The AAR leader should make a concerted effort to draw in and include soldiers who seem reluctant to participate" [V] (Ch 4, "Introduction and Rules"). The leader's techniques are specified: "Enter the discussion only when necessary; Reinforce the fact that it is permissible to disagree; Focus on learning and encourage people to give honest opinions; Use open-ended and leading questions to guide the discussion of soldier, leader, and unit performance" [V] (Ch 4, "Introduction and Rules").

### Review of objectives and intent

The AAR leader restates the unit training objectives plus the tasks being reviewed and their conditions and standards (Ch 4, "Training Objectives"). The commander then restates the mission and his intent using maps, operational graphics, and terrain boards. "Another technique is to have subordinate leaders restate the mission and discuss their commander's intent" [V] (Ch 4, "Commander's Mission and Intent"). In a formal AAR, the OPFOR commander then explains his plan to defeat friendly forces, using the same training aids "so participants can understand the relationship of both plans" [V] (Ch 4, "OPFOR Commander's Mission and Intent").

### Summary of recent events: open-ended questions

The TC gives explicit guidance on question form: "He should not ask yes or no questions, but encourage participation and guide discussion by using open-ended and leading questions. An open-ended question has no specific answer and allows the person answering to reply based on what was significant to him. Open-ended questions are also much less likely to put him on the defensive. This is more effective in finding out what happened" [V] (Ch 4, "Summary of Recent Events"). The TC includes a worked contrast:

> "'SGT Johnson, what happened when your Bradley crested the hill?'
>
> rather than-
>
> 'SGT Johnson, why didn't you engage the enemy tanks to your front?'"
> (Ch 4, "Summary of Recent Events")

The first form invites narrative; the second form invites self-defence. The TC adds: "As the discussion expands and more soldiers add their perspectives, what really happened will become clear. Remember, this is not a critique or lecture; the OC does not tell the soldiers or leaders what was good or bad. However, the AAR leader must ensure specific issues are revealed, both positive and negative in nature. Skillful guidance of the discussion will ensure the AAR does not gloss over mistakes or unit weaknesses" [V] (Ch 4, "Summary of Recent Events").

### Discussion of key issues: three techniques

"The AAR is a problem-solving process. The purpose of discussion is for participants to discover strengths and weaknesses, propose solutions, and adopt a course of action to correct problems. Leaders can organize the discussion using one of the three techniques in the following paragraphs" [V] (Ch 4, "Discussion of Key Issues").

The three techniques:

1. **Chronological order of events.** "This technique is logical, structured, and easy to understand. It follows the flow of training from start to finish and allows soldiers to see the effects of their actions on other units and events. By covering actions in the order they took place, soldiers and leaders are better able to recall what happened" [V] (Ch 4, "Chronological Order of Events").

2. **Battlefield Operating Systems (BOS).** "To focus and structure the AAR, the leader can also use the seven BOS (Figure 4-2). By focusing on each BOS and discussing it across all phases of the training exercise, participants can identify systemic strengths and weaknesses. This technique is particularly useful in training staff sections whose duties and responsibilities directly relate to one or more BOS. However, leaders using this technique must be careful not to lose sight of the big picture. They must not get into long discussions about BOS which do not relate to mission accomplishment" [V] (Ch 4, "Battlefield Operating Systems (BOS)"). The seven BOS are: Intelligence (INTEL), Maneuver (MVR), Fire Support (FS), Mobility/countermobility/survivability (M, C, S), Air Defense (AD), Combat Service Support (CSS), and Command and Control (C²) [V] (Ch 4, Figure 4-2).

3. **Key events/themes/issues.** "A key events discussion focuses on critical training events which directly support training objectives the chain of command identified before the exercise began. Keeping a tight focus on these events prevents the discussion from becoming sidetracked by issues which do not relate to training objectives. This technique is particularly effective when time is limited" [V] (Ch 4, "Key Events/Themes/Issues").

### Fratricide as a mandatory deep-discussion topic

The TC isolates fratricide for explicit mandatory treatment: "All incidents or near incidents of fratricide, whether inflicted by direct fire, indirect fire, or close air support (CAS), will be discussed in detail. The leader must focus on identifying the cause of the fratricide and develop SOPs and TTPs to prevent it in the future. Regardless of the environment (training or combat), the leader must swiftly deal with all fratricide incidents. As soon as possible after the event, an AAR should be held to discuss the circumstances surrounding the event, using the following discussion points: How and why did the incident occur? How were friendly personnel and equipment identified? What fire control measures were in place where the fratricide occurred and how effective were they? How did the commander's risk assessment and overall intent for the mission address the issue of fratricide?" [V] (Ch 4, "Fratricide").

### Flexibility and discussion discipline

"One of the strengths of the AAR format is its flexibility. The leader could use the chronological format to structure the discussion, then, if a particular BOS seems to have systemic issues that the group needs to address, follow that BOS across the entire exercise. Once that topic is exhausted, the AAR could proceed using the chronological format" [V] (Ch 4, "Flexibility"). The leader must: "Be specific, avoiding generalizations; Be thorough; Not dwell on issues unrelated to mission accomplishment; Focus on actions; Relate performance to the accomplishment of training objectives; Identify corrective action for areas of weakness; Continually summarize" [V] (Ch 4, "Flexibility").

### Optional discussion topics

Soldier and leader skills: "Through discussion, the unit can identify critical soldier and leader skills which affected unit or individual performance" [V] (Ch 4, "Soldier/Leader Skills"). Leader-skills discussions are often best held in a separate AAR: "This allows for a candid discussion of leadership issues without wasting unit AAR time best spent on reviewing the entire training exercise. The AAR leader for follow-on meetings should be a member of the unit so participants can candidly address key training issues without fear of airing dirty laundry in front of outsiders" [V] (Ch 4, "Soldier/Leader Skills").

Tasks to sustain or improve: focus retraining on mission-essential tasks and supporting tasks that need improvement rather than training to known strengths. The TC's directive form: "Train to weakness, not to strength" [V] (Ch 4, "Tasks to Sustain/Improve").

Statistics: "Statistics is a double-edged sword. Effective feedback requires participants to measure, collect, and quantify performance during the training exercise. Statistics supply objective facts which reinforce observations of both strengths and weaknesses. The danger lies in statistics for statistics' sake. Chart after chart of ratios, bar graphs, and tables quickly obscures any meaning and lends itself to a 'grading' of unit performance. This stifles discussion and degrades the AAR's value. Statistics and statistics-based charts should *identify critical trends or issues and reinforce teaching points*. (An example for an armored unit would be to link the number of rounds fired to the number of enemy vehicles destroyed. This would provide a good indication of unit gunnery skills.) Judicious use of statistic feedback supports observations and provides a focus to AAR discussions" [V] (Ch 4, "Statistics").

Force protection (safety) is to be specifically addressed in every AAR: "Safety is every soldier's business and applies to everything a unit does in the field and in garrison. Safety should be specifically addressed in every AAR and discussed in detail when it impacts unit effectiveness or soldier health. The important thing is to treat safety precautions as integral parts of every operation" [V] (Ch 4, "Discussion of Force Protection (Safety) Issues").

### Closing comments

"During the summary, the AAR leader reviews and summarizes key points identified during the discussion. He should end the AAR on a positive note, linking conclusions to future training. He should then leave the immediate area to allow unit leaders and soldiers time to discuss the training in private" [V] (Ch 4, "Closing Comments").

## Part V: Following up (Ch 5)

### Benefits and the link to METL proficiency

"The real benefits of AARs come from taking the results and applying them to future training. Leaders can use the information to assess performance and to immediately retrain units in tasks where there is weakness. Leaders can also use AARs to help assess unit METL proficiency" [V] (Ch 5, "Benefits"). Immediately or shortly after the training event, leaders should conduct a *trained-practiced-untrained (T-P-U) assessment* and develop a future-training concept [V] (Ch 5, "Benefits"). Retraining should not be delayed or rescheduled "except when absolutely necessary. If the leader delays retraining, he must be sure the soldiers understand that they did not perform the task to standard and that retraining will occur later" [V] (Ch 5, "Benefits").

The TC frames the cycle: "After-action reviews are the dynamic link between task performance and execution to standard. They provide commanders a critical assessment tool to use to plan soldier, leader, and unit training. Through the professional and candid discussion of events, soldiers can compare their performance against the standard and identify specific ways to improve proficiency" [V] (Ch 5, "Benefits").

### Immediate retraining and critical gate tasks

"Retraining may be immediately necessary to address particularly weak areas. By applying its learning, a unit can improve its performance to meet the Army standard. However, the focus of this effort is not to get an A or B; it is to improve soldier and unit performance. By the end of an AAR, soldiers must clearly understand what was good, bad, or average about their performance" [V] (Ch 5, "Immediate Retraining"). FM 25-100 and FM 25-101 require leaders to schedule time for retraining as a normal part of the planning process: "The unit must retrain on the tasks which they did not perform to standard before the unit can go to the next training event. The unit must always retrain and perform critical gate tasks derived from the Combined Arms Training Strategy (CATS) to standard before progressing to the next level of tasks" [V] (Ch 5, "Immediate Retraining").

Critical gate tasks are defined: "Critical gate tasks are tasks grouped in a training event that a soldier or unit *must* perform and receive an evaluation for before progressing to more complex or difficult tasks or events. Commanders *must* prescribe the performance of the task to standard as a prerequisite for progressing to subsequent tasks or events" [V] (Ch 5, "Immediate Retraining").

### Revised SOPs

"After-action reviews may reveal problems with unit SOPs. If so, unit leaders must revise the SOP and make sure units implement the changes during any future training" [V] (Ch 5, "Revised Standing Operating Procedures").

### The AAR in combat

"Training does not stop when a unit goes into combat. Training is *always* an integral part of precombat and combat operations although limited time and proximity to the enemy may restrict the type and extent of training. Only training improves combat performance without imposing the stiff penalties combat inflicts on the untrained" [V] (Ch 5, "The After-Action Review in Combat"). The TC argues the AAR is well-suited to combat: "The AAR is one of the most effective techniques to use in a combat environment. An effective AAR takes little time, and leaders can conduct them almost anywhere consistent with unit security requirements. Conducting AARs helps overcome the steep learning curve that exists in a unit exposed to combat and helps the unit ensure that it does not repeat mistakes. It also helps them sustain strengths. By integrating training into combat operations and using tools such as AARs, leaders can dramatically increase their unit's chances for success on the battlefield" [V] (Ch 5, "The After-Action Review in Combat").

## Part VI: AAR techniques from CTC observers and controllers (Appendix A)

Appendix A draws on AAR practice at the Army's Combat Training Centers (CTCs). The framing: "Observers and controllers from combat training centers (CTCs) administer AARs every day. The experience of professional AAR leaders yields techniques that apply to all units, not just those that rotate through CTCs" [V] (App A, opening).

### Site selection and improvement

"After-action review leaders should select sites where a free exchange of ideas and discussion of recent events can take place. An appropriate site would overlook areas of significant action, such as a hill overlooking an obstacle breach. The importance of selecting a site where participants can observe the actual locations where key events took place cannot be overstated" [V] (App A, "Site Selection"). Environmental factors matter: "When possible, avoid seating soldiers in direct sunlight, excessive darkness, or exposing them to rain or snow. When weather is particularly bad, an alternate AAR site, such as a building, bunker, range tower, or possibly even a tent, would be appropriate" [V] (App A, "Site Selection").

Site improvement: "If there is no way to avoid direct sunlight, the leader should erect some type of covering, such as a camouflage net or a tarp. Cover from direct sunlight allows soldiers to concentrate on the task at hand. Likewise, protecting soldiers from heavy rains or snow has a positive effect. The leader can counteract high winds by placing a vehicle on the up-wind side or by moving soldiers to an area with sufficient cover" [V] (App A, "Site Improvement"). Cost-benefit consideration is built in: "Some sites lend themselves to rapid improvement. Others require a large investment in time and energy to make them suitable. The AAR leader must carefully weigh the costs and benefits of the site. The prudent decision may be to move to an area which may not overlook the terrain but which does facilitate a good AAR" [V] (App A, "Site Improvement").

### Training aids by application

Figure A-1 lists seven categories of common training aids by application: tape recorder (radio traffic, OPORDs); video tape recorder + TV monitor (record key events for playback); dry-erase board (portable chalkboard); butcher paper (outlines, teaching points, agenda); map overlay (guide discussion of events, bigger than 1:50,000 if possible); terrain model (greater detail than with a map); model vehicles and toy soldiers (simulate soldiers and equipment on terrain models) [V] (App A, Figure A-1).

The TC explicitly rejects elaboration for its own sake: "Training aids need not be complicated or fancy to be effective. For example, instead of butcher paper or a dry-erase board, a poncho or the side of a tracked vehicle would do. If a terrain model is needed, use the same one that the unit used for the operation, if possible" [V] (App A, "Training Aids"). The selection criterion is operational fit: "The best training aids are those that enhance learning without distracting from the AAR's overall goal. Leaders must carefully select only those training aids which directly apply to key events. For example, it would be appropriate to have a detailed scale terrain model when conducting an AAR of a squad ambush. However, a detailed scale terrain model is probably not appropriate when conducting an AAR for marksmanship training. The key is, if it does not assist, then it detracts" [V] (App A, "Training Aids").

### Site organisation: leader position, seating, training aids

The AAR discussion leader should be "centrally located and stand directly facing the majority of the soldiers taking part. If the AAR is for a large group, the discussion leader should be elevated" [V] (App A, "Organization of the AAR Site").

Seating: "Seating should be in a horseshoe shape when possible. This allows the maximum number of soldiers to have 'front row seats' with no one standing behind the discussion leader. The leader of the unit undergoing the AAR should be seated in the front and center of the horseshoe. Higher echelon leaders and commanders should be seated to the flanks and rear of the horseshoe" [V] (App A, "Organization of the AAR Site"). The TC's worked example uses a platoon AAR: "the platoon leader and platoon sergeant should be seated in the front and center of the horseshoe, with their soldiers spread out to the left and right. The company commander and battalion commander would be located behind the back row of soldiers. This allows soldiers taking part in the AAR to feel that their comments are valued as much as those of senior leaders. It is important that the atmosphere of the AAR be one of open discussion, not one stifled by rank consciousness" [V] (App A, "Organization of the AAR Site").

Training aids placement: "Training aids must be centrally located so the maximum number of soldiers can benefit from their use. Terrain boards should be propped at an angle to allow greater visibility. Television monitors, if used, should be placed out of direct light. Butcher-paper charts and dry-erase boards work best when everyone can see them. However, they must not dominate the AAR. When using butcher-paper charts, the leader should be sure printing is in the block style and large enough for everyone to read" [V] (App A, "Organization of the AAR Site").

## Key planning targets (with provenance)

| Item | Value | Classification |
|---|---|---|
| AAR planning lead time | 6–8 weeks before execution (formal) | [V] (Ch 1, "Formal"; Ch 2, "After-Action Review Plan") |
| Platoon-level AAR duration | approximately 30–45 minutes | [V] (Ch 2, "Scheduling Stopping Points") |
| Company-level AAR duration | about 1 hour | [V] (Ch 2, "Scheduling Stopping Points") |
| Battalion-level and above AAR duration | about 2 hours | [V] (Ch 2, "Scheduling Stopping Points") |
| Recommended seating arrangement | Horseshoe | [V] (Ch 3, "Prepare the AAR Site"; App A, "Organization of the AAR Site") |
| External evaluator echelon distance from unit being evaluated | Two echelons above | [V] (Ch 2, "After-Action Review Plan") |
| Recommended map-overlay scale (when used) | Bigger than 1:50,000 if possible | [V] (App A, Figure A-1) |
| Mandatory deep-discussion topic | Fratricide (all incidents and near-incidents) | [V] (Ch 4, "Fratricide") |
| Mandatory mention every AAR | Force protection (safety) | [V] (Ch 4, "Discussion of Force Protection") |
| Number of BOS in the seven-BOS frame | 7 (INTEL, MVR, FS, M/C/S, AD, CSS, C²) | [V] (Ch 4, Figure 4-2) |
| Three discussion-organisation techniques | Chronological / BOS / Key events | [V] (Ch 4, "Discussion of Key Issues") |
| Four AAR-process steps | Planning, Preparing, Conducting, Following up | [V] (Ch 1, "After-Action Review Planning and Execution Sequence") |
| Post-training assessment categories | Trained / Practiced / Untrained (T-P-U) | [V] (Ch 5, "Benefits") |

## Connections the text makes

- **FM 25-100, *Training the Force* (November 1988).** Cited as the parent doctrine for training planning, including the requirement to schedule retraining as a normal part of the planning process [BT] (Ch 5, "Immediate Retraining"; References).
- **FM 25-101, *Battle Focused Training* (September 1990).** Identified as the doctrine this TC supplements and expands: "This training circular (TC) is a leader's guide on how to plan, prepare, and conduct an AAR. It supplements and expands the guidance in Field Manual (FM) 25-101" [V] (Preface). Appendix G of FM 25-101 is referenced for additional AAR detail [BT] (App A, closing).
- **ARTEP mission training plans (AMTPs) and soldier's manuals.** Cited as the source of training-and-evaluation outlines (TEOs) that provide tasks, conditions, and standards [BT] (Ch 2, "Reviewing the Training and Evaluation Plan"; Ch 3, "Review Doctrine").
- **Combined Arms Training Strategy (CATS).** Cited as the source from which critical gate tasks are derived [BT] (Ch 5, "Immediate Retraining").
- **Combat training centers (CTCs).** Cited in Appendix A as the source of professional AAR practice that the TC distils into general technique [BT] (App A, opening).

## Positions the text explicitly frames against

- **The AAR as critique.** The TC explicitly opposes the critique frame: a critique gives one viewpoint, suppresses discussion, focuses only on what is wrong, and "stifles learning and team building" [V] (Ch 1, "Definition and Purpose"). The AAR by contrast is "not a critique" and "does not grade success or failure" [V] (Ch 4, "Introduction and Rules").
- **The AAR as a grading exercise.** "An AAR does not grade success or failure. There are always weaknesses to improve and strengths to sustain" [V] (Ch 4, "Introduction and Rules"). Statistics misuse is explicitly named: "Chart after chart of ratios, bar graphs, and tables quickly obscures any meaning and lends itself to a 'grading' of unit performance. This stifles discussion and degrades the AAR's value" [V] (Ch 4, "Statistics").
- **Yes/no questions during the AAR.** "He should not ask yes or no questions" [V] (Ch 4, "Summary of Recent Events"). The TC's worked contrast (Bradley/hill versus engage/tanks-to-front) makes the distinction concrete.
- **Training to known strengths.** "Train to weakness, not to strength" [V] (Ch 4, "Tasks to Sustain/Improve"). The TC frames training to known strengths as a wasted-cycles failure mode.
- **Rank consciousness suppressing discussion.** The seating arrangement deliberately positions higher-echelon leaders to the flanks and rear of the horseshoe so junior soldiers' comments are not pre-empted: "It is important that the atmosphere of the AAR be one of open discussion, not one stifled by rank consciousness" [V] (App A, "Organization of the AAR Site").
- **Treating AARs as cure-alls.** "Of course, AARs are not cure-alls for unit-training problems. Leaders must still make on-the-spot corrections and take responsibility for training their soldiers and units" [V] (Ch 1, "Definition and Purpose").
- **Gadgetry for its own sake.** "[H]e should not get carried away with gadgets when a pencil and paper would do" [V] (Ch 3, "Observe the Training and Take Notes"). "Training aids need not be complicated or fancy to be effective" [V] (App A, "Training Aids").

## Citation and source-integrity notes

PDF source converted with `pymupdf4llm 0.2.9` in 3.2 seconds (35 pages). Conversion preserved chapter and section headers cleanly; tables (Figure 2-1 exercise AAR plan, Figure 2-2 training aids, Figure A-1 training-aid applications) survived as markdown tables. Figures 1-1 (AAR Key Points), 1-2 (AAR Format), 1-3 (Types of AARs), 1-4 (The AAR Process), 3-3 (Typical AAR Site) are referenced by name in the converted markdown but their diagram content is not text-extracted; the body prose surrounding each figure carries the substantive claim and is the deep-reference anchor. Figure 4-1 (Sequence for Conducting AARs) was carried in the source as a text outline rather than a diagram and is captured verbatim in Part IV above. Image classification was deferred for this ingestion (text-only scope).

The TC's prose is consistently masculine-pronoun ("the OC … he"; "the leader … he"); a Preface note states: "Unless this publication states otherwise, masculine nouns and pronouns refer to both men and women" (Preface). Verbatim quotations preserve this usage faithfully without modernisation; the deep reference's own prose uses gender-neutral framing where it speaks in its own voice.

Citation style is `(Ch N, "Section name")`. Section names follow the TC's own ALL-CAPS headers (here normalised to title case for readability). Where a section name is ambiguous (e.g. Ch 4 has three nested discussion-technique subsections), citations name the immediate subsection (e.g. "Chronological Order of Events" rather than the parent "Discussion of Key Issues").

The TC has no bibliography beyond the two-entry References page (FM 25-100, FM 25-101). All claims in this deep reference trace to either the TC's own prose, its figures, or its glossary; there are no borrowed-through claims that require external corroboration. The text presents itself as doctrine, not as empirical research — there are no statistical claims, no studies cited, no datasets, no comparative evidence offered or required.
